title: Roma Civilization_The History of Rome, Books 37 to the End by Livy
paragraphs: Lucius Cornelius Scipio and Caius Laelius, as consuls, conducted no business in the senate before attending to religious duties. Their ambassadors were pressing because they had a short truce with the Ætolians, but they also received support from Titus Quinctius, who had recently returned from Greece. After several days of debate, it was finally decided that neither peace nor war would be granted or refused.

When this plan, either original or supported by precedents of a record now lost by antiquity, was referred to the senate, it had aroused them by the expectation of a contest. Publius Scipio Africanus said, "If they decreed that province to my brother, Lucius Scipio, I would go along with him as his lieutenant-general." "This proposal was met with universal approval and brought an end to all disputes." The prodigies were repaid, and the Latin festival was celebrated for the second time, as the meat intended for the Laurentians had not been distributed to them. Publius Cornelius Scipio Africanus erected an arch on the Capitol before leaving the city. The arch faced the road leading up to it and featured seven gilded statues and two horses. In front of the arch, he placed two marble cisterns. During that period, forty-three of the principal Ætolians, including Damocritus and his brother, were brought to Rome by two cohorts sent by Manius Acilius. They were imprisoned in the Lautumiæ prison, also known as the quarry.

Lucius Cornelius, the consul, instructed his cohorts to return to the army. The ambassadors who came from Ptolemy and Cleopatra, the king and queen of Egypt, congratulated the Romans because the consul Manius Acilius had driven King Antiochus out of Greece. They advised that the Romans should carry their army into Asia. At the time the consul set out to war, he did so during the celebration of the Apollinarian games on the fifth day before the ides of July. The sky was serene, but the light was obscured in the middle of the day as the moon passed beneath the orb of the sun.

To Lucius Aurunculeius, the Senate assigned the task of building thirty quinqueremes and twenty triremes. This decision followed a report that Antiochus had been fitting out a significantly larger fleet since his engagement at sea. The Ætolians, after ambassadors brought back word from Rome indicating there was no hope of peace, seized on Mount Corax, regarding the danger more than their losses. Their whole sea-coast, opposite Peloponnesus, had been ravaged by the Achæans. They sought to shut up the pass against the Romans, as they expected them to return in the spring to the siege of Naupactum. It seemed wiser for Acilius, who knew this was expected, to undertake an unanticipated endeavor and lay siege to Lamia. The garrison had been reduced by Philip to a state of desperation; thus, being then off their guard because they feared no such attempt, might be caught off balance by himself.

As was usual in an unexpected affair, great consternation and tumult ensued; yet the besieged, with greater resolution than any one could suppose them capable of under such a sudden alarm, when men fought and women brought weapons of every kind, including stones, to defend the city for that day. After their bodies had been refreshed by food and rest, he dismissed the meeting in the Prætorium. Before doing so, he gave them notice: "Be ready and under arms before daybreak; you are not to return to your tents until the city has been taken." The next day, at the same hour as before, the assault was launched in more locations than previously. As the garrison's strength, weapons, and most importantly, its courage began to wane, the town fell within a short span of time. The Ætolians occupied the pass at Corax while no one approved of defying the terms of Naupactum.

That, however, the summer campaign may not be idle, and that the Aetolians might not possess the peace through his supineness, which they could not obtain from the senate, Acilius resolved to besiege Amphissa; his army was led thither from Heraclea by Œtus. The wall was breached in several locations as news of his death spread. His successor, who had landed his army at Apollonia, was marching through Epirus and Thessaly. He had already arrived at the Malian bay. Upon receiving a response from his messengers sent ahead to Hypata, ordering a surrender of the city, he was told that the Ætolians would do nothing except by common consent. This allowed him to avoid detaining himself at Hypata while Amphissa was still recovering. Having dispatched his brother Africanus to lead his forces there, he turned his attention to Amphissa.

The consul pitched his camp six miles from the town. Ambassadors from Athens arrived, addressing Publius Scipio first, as previously noted, followed by the consul, who made urgent appeals on behalf of the Aetolians. By the advice of the Athenians, a large embassy from Aetolia arrived quickly from Hypata. The discussion with Africanus, whom they first addressed, boosted their hopes for peace. He mentioned that many nations and states, starting in Spain and then in Africa, had sought his protection. In all of them, he had left behind greater monuments to clemency and kindness than to military achievements. The consul, upon being asked, reiterated the response that had previously repelled the senate. When the Aetolians were thunder-struck by this, as if they had never heard it before, for they now perceived that no progress had been made either from the Athenian embassy or the favorable reply of Africanus, they said they wished to consult their countrymen on the matter. 7

They therefore ordered the same ambassadors to return to the consul and Africanus, and to request that if they truly meant to grant them peace, rather than merely tantalizing them with a prospect of it, and thus crushing the hopes of the wretched, they would either remit some part of the money required to be paid or order that the unconditional submission should not extend to their persons. The king's disposition should be the first thing to be discovered.

Antiochus, after the sea-fight at Corycus, was chiefly intent on refitting his ships during the entire winter, when he had a clear period to carry out his preparations both by land and water. A sedition broke out at Phocaea, as a result of efforts to win over the crowd to the side of Antiochus. The winter quarters of the ships were a heavy burden. The tribute imposed on them was equally burdensome, as they had to provide five hundred gowns and five hundred tunics. Additionally, the scarcity of corn made the situation even more unbearable, prompting the evacuation of both the ships and the Roman garrison from the area.

By order of the mother of the gods, the immediate servants of the goddess came to pray the Roman commander to spare the walls and the city. None of them were injured; and, presently, the whole senate and the magistrates came out to surrender the place. While these transactions take place at the Hellespont, Polyxenidas, commander of the king's fleet (though an exile from Rhodes), had heard that ships from his homeland had set sail and that Pausistratus, who commanded them, had made several disparaging remarks about him in a public speech. This sparked jealousy in Polyxenidas, who became fixated on proving himself against Pausistratus' boasts. He sent part of his ships to Halicarnassus to bring provisions and another part to the city of Samos. He remained at Panormus, waiting for the signal to launch an attack, which would come when the traitor signaled him to do so.

Polyxenidas, having fully adjusted all his measures, called in the rowers from Magnesia, and launched hastily the ships that were in dock by night. He had wasted the day not so much in preparation as because he was unwilling that the fleet should be seen going to sea. Set sail after sunset with seventy decked ships, but the wind being contrary, he put into the harbour of Pygelia before daylight. When he had rested during the day, for the same reason as before, he crossed over into the nearest part of Samian territory at night. Pausistratus was initially confused by the unexpected event, but after regaining his composure as an old soldier, he judged that the enemy would be more easily defeated on land than at sea. He then marched his armed forces in two bodies towards the promontories, which jutted out into the deep water, forming a natural harbour. Pausistratus believed that the only way to ensure safety was to force through the narrow entrance of the port and push out into the open sea. After that, he saw his men being embarked, ordering the rest to follow. He himself led the way, with the ship urged vigorously by the oars, pressed towards the mouth of the harbour.

Some ships were taken outside the harbour, while others remained inside. Others were claimed by Nicander as they disembarked from the shore. The galleys of Erythrae, after meeting not far from Samos, encountered the Rhodian ships, which were fleeing and came to seek refuge. They then sailed away towards the Romans in the direction of the Hellespont. While these events were taking place in Aetolia, after Abydos, which was defended by a garrison of the king's troops, had endured a siege for several days, all parties then grew weary of the struggle, and the magistrates, with Philotas' permission as commander of the garrison, began to negotiate with Livius regarding the terms on which they would surrender the city.

The king's troops were unable to decide whether to march out with their arms or without them. This indecision prolonged the issue. Afterwards, because they had been betrayed by treachery, and by one of their own countrymen, their grief was transformed into anger. After the storm dispersed the fleet, he thought it a good opportunity to attack them. However, in a short time, the wind increased and raised a heavy sea, making it impossible for him to approach them. He steered towards the island of Æthalia, where he could launch an attack on the ships the following day as they sailed from the main sea towards Samos.

As the sun set, a few Roman ships arrived at a harbor on the Samian coast. The remaining vessels, battered by the stormy night, followed suit and entered the same harbor. The Rhodian fleet arrived at the same location after several days. To which place did Timasicrates, a Rhodian, come in the middle of the night with two quadriremes from Samos? He was presented to Æmilius and told him he had been sent to escort him safely. The king's ships frequently sailed from the Hellespont and Abydos, making the sea on that coast hazardous for transports. Two Rhodian quadriremes met Æmilius on his passage from Chios to Samos. They were sent by Livius to accompany him. King Eumenes also arrived with two quinqueremes. Amidst his arrival on Samos, as soon as he received the command of the fleet from Livius, and having fulfilled the customary rituals, Amilius convened a council.

Caius Livius, whose opinion was first sought, replied that "no one could offer advice with greater sincerity than he, who recommended another's actions when he himself would take the same course in similar circumstances." He noted that the narrow passage could be more easily blocked off because its entrance resembled a river, being long and narrow, and littered with shoals. However, if they were to remain there, what benefit would come from closing the harbour?

When he arrived in Rhodes, he explained his mission to the authorities, who were informed about the purpose of his visit. As the wind shifted, the sea began to churn with heavy waves. They rowed tirelessly until they reached land, but found no safe harbor. The road was impassable due to the turbulent sea, and night was approaching. They therefore sailed past the city to the port of Phoenicus, which was not quite two miles away. The port offered shelter from the violent waves, but high cliffs loomed over it. The town's people, joined by the king's troops stationed there, immediately seized control.

After that, greater numbers flocked thither from the city, and at length, the whole multitude pouring out, fear seized Livius, not only that the auxiliaries might be cut off, but that the ships would be in danger from the land. However, the fight was doubtful; and, besides a considerable number of soldiers, Lucius Apustius fell in this disorderly combat. After they replied that nothing was in their power, he advanced to lay siege to the city.

At headquarters, a private conversation arose first among the tribunes of the soldiers, and later reached Æmilius' ears. The fleet was departing to a distance from Ephesus, away from the war that concerned them. As a result, the enemy was left behind without control, allowing them to make whatever attempts they pleased against the many states of the allies in their neighbourhood. In the meantime, Seleucus, son of Antiochus, who had kept his army in Aetolia, spent the winter partly supporting his allies and partly ravaging the lands of those he could not win over to his side. Resolved to make an incursion into the territory of King Eumenes, while he was at a great distance from home, assisting the Romans and Rhodians in attacks on the maritime parts of Lycia. He advanced with hostile standards, first, to Elaea. Afterwards, having decided to abandon the plan to besiege Elaea due to the wasteful nature of the campaign, he led his army to lay siege to Pergamum, the capital and principal fortress of the kingdom.

After some time, it became clear that he was no match for the enemy in any respect. He retreated his men within the fortifications, after which the city was besieged. Antiochus, leaving Apamea with a large army composed of various nations, set up camp first at Sardis and later established himself at a nearby location, just outside the camp of Seleucus, near the River Caicus. In a few days, the combined fleet of the Romans and Rhodians arrived from Samos to Elæa, to support the king.

The Rhodians were not averse to a pacification. However, Eumenes maintained that "it was not honorable to discuss peace at that time, and no end could be put to the matter." "For," said he, "how can we, shut up as we are, within our walls, and besieged, accept terms of peace." As they entered, they became trapped, and with fear, they confined themselves within the walls. A contempt arose among the king's troops, leading to negligence. The greater number did not keep their horses either saddled or bridled. Few remained under arms, and in the ranks. The rest slipped away, scattering themselves everywhere over the entire plain. Some diverted themselves to youthful sports and amusements, while others ate in the shade, and some lay stretched out on the ground asleep.

The people in Pergamum believed it was madness, not bravery, to take such a bold move. The enemy, after observing his party for a short time, remained indifferent when they saw no movement among them and continued to mock the small size of their group. Diophanes kept his men quiet for a long time, as if they had been brought out merely to survey their surroundings. However, as soon as he detected that the enemy had abandoned their positions, he ordered the infantry to follow closely behind. With his own troop leading the way, Diophantes charged forward at the head of the cavalry, pushing on with all possible speed. As he approached, a shout was raised by every horseman and footman simultaneously. Diophantes pursued the fugitives as far as he could with safety, and then returned to the garrison, having acquired great honor for the Achaean nation. For both men and women had watched from the walls of Pergamum. 21

They had been waiting for hours, both parties anticipating the attack. He subsequently captured Peræa, a colony of Mityleneans. The cities of Cotton, Corylenus, Aphrodisias, and Crene fell to him during the initial assault. When they, having divided the quarters among themselves, assaulted it, and saw that it could not be taken by arms and scaling-ladders, without regular works; a reinforcement of three thousand soldiers sent by Antiochus arrived at the city. They immediately broke up the siege, and the fleet retreated to the island, having achieved nothing more than the devastation of the enemy's country in the surrounding area. 22

After his obsequies were performed, the Rhodians sailed to Rhodes with thirteen of their own ships, one Coan and one Cnidian quinquereme. The rear division had no room left beside the land; and, while they were in disarray, the battle was already underway on the right against Hannibal. 24 A Rhodian vessel sank one of the king's ships with a single blow. The right wing of the enemy was now undoubtedly on the verge of defeat.

Hannibal and his army fled on foot, as they had no divisions left to fight. The Rhodians gave up the chase, as many of their rowers were too ill to continue. Eudamus's ship was damaged in several areas. He therefore ordered Pamphilidas and Chariclitus to pursue as far as they deemed safe. They accordingly pursued for a considerable time. After that, Hannibal steered close to the land, fearing to be wind-bound on an enemy's coast. They then steered back to Eudamus and with difficulty towed a captured seven-banked ship to Phaselis, which had been damaged at the beginning of the engagement.

They then sailed home to Rhodes, not so much celebrating their victory as blaming each other for not taking or sinking the entire fleet, which they could have done. They sent Pamphilidas with four ships to join the fleet at Patara. Antiochus collected taxes from the surrounding states, but also sent an embassy to Prusias, king of Bithynia, along with a letter, in which he complained about the Roman pressure on Asia. "They were coming," he said, "to abolish all kingly governments. There would be no empire left in the world, except for that of Rome."

He might have maintained his honorable position if not for his own madness and later the treachery of the Aetolians. The king's resolve was especially confirmed after Caius Livius, who had commanded the fleet as praetor, came to him as an ambassador from Rome, and informed him that the Romans had a far greater hope of success than Antiochus; and how much more sacred and lasting an alliance with them would be. 26 Antiochus, after losing hope of an alliance with Prusias, went from Sardis to Ephesus to review the fleet that was fitted out and lay there ready for several months. He did so rather because he saw it impossible, with his land forces, to make any stand against the Roman army and its commanders, the two Scipios, than that his naval force by itself had ever been tried successfully or afforded great confidence at this juncture. He was relying on these factors when he ordered Polyxenidas to attempt a naval engagement at all costs, while he led his land forces to Notium. He wished to gain control over this city, as it was close to Ephesus, making it impossible for anything on sea or land to remain hidden from the Colophonians, who would instantly inform the Romans. The latter, having heard of the siege, would likely send their fleet from Samos to aid an ally, providing Polyxenidas with a chance to act.

He therefore began to lay a regular siege to the city, making his approaches simultaneously on both sides next to the sea. In both places, he joined his engines and mounds to the wall, and brought up the rams under covered galleries. They were pirates' cutters and galleys. They had a significant advantage in speed, thanks to their lighter galleys, which were designed specifically for that purpose. Being closer to land, they managed to outrun the fleet and escaped to Myonnesus before it could catch up.

They finally gave up on this pointless endeavor just before nightfall and arrived at Teos the following day. The praetor, after anchoring his ship in the harbor behind the city, called for the soldiers to pillage the surrounding countryside. 28 It happened that Polyxenidas, who had sailed with the king's fleet from Colophon, after hearing that the Romans had left Samos and pursued the pirates to Myonnesus, and that they were laying waste to the lands of the Teians, cast anchor in a secluded harbour on an island called Macris, opposite Myonnesus. Polyxenidas intended to seize this narrow pass in the night. He planned to attack both sides of the enemy's fleet that was sailing out, while ten ships stood at each of the promontories. His plan was to land his armed men from the fleet, as he had done at Panormus, and overpower the Romans on land and sea simultaneously.

Eudamus, a Rhodian, is credited with pointing out the flaw in the outer harbor. Two ships became entangled as they navigated the narrow entrance, their oars locked together. Among other motives, this also led the praetor to remove his fleet, for there was a threat from the land, as Antiochus kept his camp nearby. 29 When the fleet was brought round to the city, all being ignorant of the approaching enemy, both soldiers and sailors went ashore to divide provisions among the ships, particularly the wine. About mid-day, a peasant was brought before the praetor, who informed him that the enemy's fleet had been anchored at the island of Macris for two days; and that some of them had recently been observed preparing to set sail.

The confusion was not less than if the place were on fire or taken by an enemy. Some ran to call out the men, while others hurried to the ships. The officers' orders were lost amidst irregular shouts and the sound of trumpets, until the crowd finally gathered at the ships. The foremost division formed under the eye of the praetor, while the rear was brought up by the Rhodians. Then the entire line, in as regular an order as if within sight of the enemy, advanced into the open sea. Those who carried fire before them were the greatest terror to the enemy. It was this that ensured their survival when they were surrounded at Panormus, proving to be a key factor in their ultimate victory.

For when the king's ships, fearing the fire, turned aside to avoid encountering the enemy's prow with their own, they could not strike their antagonist with their beaks. Instead, they exposed their sides to his strokes. If any ship dared to engage, it was immediately engulfed by the flames poured in; meanwhile, the men were more frightened by the fire than the battle itself. The bravery of the soldiers was largely decisive in determining the outcome of the battle. By Antiochus, who was terrified, because as he was driven from the possession of the sea, he despaired of defending his distant possessions. He ordered the garrison to be withdrawn from Lysimachia, a plan that proved ill-conceived, as events subsequently showed, lest it should be cut off by the Romans.

After his defeat at sea, he abandoned Lysimachia and laid siege to Colophon, before retreating to Sardis. He focused all his thoughts on a single objective: meeting the enemy in the field. He dispatched emissaries to Ariarathes in Cappadocia, requesting his assistance and gathering forces wherever possible. Amius Regillus, after his victory at sea, proceeded to Ephesus, where he had drawn up his ships before the harbor. Having extorted from the enemy a final acknowledgment of their having surrendered the dominion of the sea, he sailed to Chios, which he had directed his course towards from Samos, prior to the sea-fight. While they were busily employed in this manner, Quintus Antonius arrived, sent by the praetor. He then chastised their obstinacy and assured them that "The Romans were more eager than they were themselves to prevent the battle from being fought to the destruction of the city."

If they would desist from their madness, Amilius would allow them to surrender on the same terms on which they had previously surrendered to Caius Livius. When they heard this, they took five days to deliberate and, in the meantime, tried to seek aid from Antiochus. However, after the ambassadors who had been sent to the king returned with word that no aid was forthcoming, they opened their gates, stipulating that they would suffer nothing hostile. This event gave much more satisfaction than even the success at sea. After their arrival, they were warmly welcomed in the city, which was well-stocked with provisions of all kinds, as if intended for the arrival of an army. In the siege of the city, however, they had anticipated extreme hardship and scarcity. They afterwards remained encamped at the Hellespont for a considerable time, as it was a period too holy for marching, during which the sacred shields were moved.

Publius Scipio was separated from the army at the same festival, as the religious ceremony was more incumbent on him, since he was one of the Salian priests. He was also a source of delay, until he caught up with the rest of the army. 34 He decided not to approach the consul until he had consulted with Publius Scipio, and was instructed to do so by the king. In him he had the greatest hope. His greatness of soul and the fullness of his glory greatly inclined him towards peace. It was known to all nations that he had been a conqueror, both in Spain and afterwards in Africa. Additionally, because his son was then a prisoner with Antiochus,

Some say that at the beginning of the war, as he was traveling from Chalcis to Oreum, he was intercepted by some of the king's ships. Others claim that after the army entered Asia, he was sent with a group of Fregellans to Antiochus's camp to gather intelligence. When the cavalry launched an attack on him, he retreated and fell off his horse in the chaos. With two other horsemen nearby, he was overpowered and taken into custody by the king. If  The ambassador addressed the council, saying, "Although numerous embassies have been sent in pursuit of peace, with no success, I remain optimistic that we can achieve it. The previous ambassadors have failed to secure any concessions."

The king had already ceded Lysimachia, so that it would not be said that he possessed anything in Europe. He was now prepared to hand over all cities in Asia, just as any others, which the Romans might wish to make independent of the king's rule, since they belonged to their faction. The king should bear the entire cost of the war, as he was responsible for starting it. The ambassador, after coming to the conclusion that he could obtain no reasonable terms in the council, tried to influence the mind of Publius Scipio in private.

After leaving the passage open to Asia, and receiving not only a bridle but also a yoke, what negotiation on equality has been left to you when you must submit to orders? Laying aside for the present all further mention of peace, he turned his whole attention to preparations for war. For two days after there was quiet, neither party passed the river.

While they were employed in measuring and fortifying the camp, a body of the king's troops, consisting of three thousand chosen horse and foot, approached with great rapidity and violence. For the next four days, both armies stood in formation before their respective camps. The consul, after realizing that he had declined the contest, called a council the following day and asked for their opinion: "How should I act if Antiochus refuses to give me an opportunity to engage."

Cneius Domitius was sent to discover the nature of the ground and determine which side the enemy's rampart could be approached. Upon his return with a detailed report, it was decided that the camp would be relocated closer to the enemy's position for the next day's operations. There was no text provided for correction. For despite this, they were not supposed to be capable of withstanding the great number of the king's elephants, which were no less than fifty-four. The African elephants, however, are not able to cope with an equal number of Indians, either because they are inferior to them in size, or in unyielding courage. 40

There was a body of 16,000 men armed in the manner of the Macedonians, known as a phalanx. The king, having positioned the chariots at the front, did so because if they were placed in the rear or between the ranks, they would have to be driven through their own soldiers. For the auxiliary troops in reserve, who arrived next, were terrified by the chaos and disorder of the chariots and fled, leaving all exposed as far as the post of the mailed horsemen. The Roman cavalry, after dispersing the reserves, approached them, but they did not withstand their initial charge.

Marcus Aemilius, a military tribune and son of Marcus Lepidus, later became chief pontifex, overseeing the camp. The fugitives suffered more severe loss due to the chariots, elephants, and camels being mixed together with their own disorganized crowd. Once they had broken their ranks, they rushed headlong into each other, as if blind, and were trampled to death by the stampede of the beasts. Up to 50,000 feet and 3,000 horses were said to have been killed that day. One thousand 400 people were taken, along with 15 elephants and their drivers.

The victors, after plundering the enemy's camp, returned with a great store of spoils to their own. A herald from Antiochus requested permission from Publius Scipio, which was granted by the consul, allowing the king to dispatch his envoys. After meeting with Eumenes, whom they expected to find opposed to peace due to old disputes, they were pleasantly surprised to see him more receptive than they or the king had anticipated. They then turned their attention to Publius Scipio and, through him, the consul. A large council was granted to them at their request to declare their commission. Zeuxis said, "We have nothing to propose ourselves, but rather to inquire from you, Romans, by what means we can make amends for our king's error and obtain pardon and peace from our conquerors."

You should act with a greater and more peaceful spirit now that you have achieved this great victory, which has made you the masters of the entire world. We, Romans, possess as much as the gods have seen fit to grant us. We now offer you the same terms that we proposed to you when we were on an equal footing, at the time your proposals for peace were made, after we crossed the Hellespont, before we encountered the king's camp or army, when the outcome of war was still in doubt.

Then, towards the expenses of the war, you shall pay fifteen thousand talents of Eubœa; five hundred immediately, two thousand five hundred when the senate and people of Rome have ratified the peace, and one thousand annually for twelve years afterwards. We demand this above all. The king will now make peace under worse circumstances on his side, because he makes it later than he might have done.

It was resolved that ambassadors should be sent to Rome. A few days later, the king brought the hostages to Ephesus to meet with the consul. The ambassadors who were also going to Rome arrived soon after. After hearing their services, the senate refused a triumph to Minucius but, with great unanimity, decreed one for Acilius. He rode through the city in triumph over King Antiochus and the Aetolians.

As the time of the consular elections approached, Caius Lælius returned to Rome from Gaul. When the Ætolian ambassadors were brought before the senate, despite their cause and circumstances requiring them to humbly seek pardon for what was either their misfortune or fault, they instead began by enumerating their services to the Roman people and speaking reproachfully of their own valour in the war with Philip. This insolence offended the senators' ears. Forsooth, the war should be waged against such determined foes, and their proud hearts tempered. Another circumstance fueled the resentment of the senate, as they sought peace with the Romans while waging war against Dolopia and Athamania.

A report of transactions in Asia spread without an author, but certain information and a letter from the general arrived at Rome soon after. This news caused joy, not just due to recent fears, as they no longer feared the conqueror in Ætolia. Instead, it was because he had started this war that they considered him a formidable enemy, both for his own strength and because he had Hannibal directing the war effort. After a short time, Marcus Aurelius Cotta, deputy of Lucius Scipio, arrived in Rome with ambassadors from King Antiochus and King Eumenes, as well as representatives from Rhodes. The senators, in turn, presented the services rendered in Asia.

This And so my speech will be all the more difficult on this account, because their demands will be of such a nature that they will appear not only to make no demands which may be contrary to my interests, but even to request nothing that is intimately connected with themselves. For they will plead the cause of the Greek states, arguing that they should be set free. If this point is achieved, it is uncertain whether they will sever ties with not only the newly liberated states but also our ancient tributaries. Given the significant obligations we have imposed on them, it is unlikely that they will maintain their status as allies in reality, while being subject to their government and entirely at their disposal. If nothing was given to me, I would return nothing.

Be cautious, for this speech may deceive you. Be wary of an uneven distribution, lest it depress some of your allies excessively while elevating others to an unfair degree. This could lead to placing those who fought against you in a better position than your own allies and friends. He did not merely show a faithful and kind inclination towards you, but was actively involved in all the wars you fought in Greece, whether on land or sea. He provided you with various provisions in such a way that none of your allies could match him in any respect. While exhorting the Bœotians to form an alliance with you, he suddenly collapsed during his speech and died shortly thereafter. I will not boast about this matter, as I was not at fault in any way that would have caused harm to you. However, I will instead highlight the instances of behavior that demonstrate the true spirit of our long-standing friendship between our families.

After liberating myself from the siege, although Antiochus and Seleucus were encamped around the citadel of my kingdom in different parts, I left my own affairs behind and sailed with my entire fleet to the Hellespont to meet your consul Lucius Scipio, to assist in transporting his army. He faithfully adhered to your cause in Africa, against Syphax and the Carthaginians.
